claim: For all integers n, if n is prime and n > 3 then n^2 ≡ 1 (mod 24).
For all integers n, if n is prime and n > 3 then n^2 ≡ 1 (mod 8).
For all integers n, if n is prime and n > 3 then n^2 ≡ 1 (mod 3).
For all integers n, if n is prime and n > 3, then n ≡ ±1 (mod 8) and n ≢ 0 (mod 3).
For all integers n, if n is prime and n > 3 then n is odd.
For all integers n, if n is prime and n > 3 then n^2 - 1 is divisible by both 8 and 3.
For all integers n, if n is prime and n > 3 then n^2 is odd.
For all integers n, if n is prime and n > 3 then (n-1)(n+1) ≡ 0 (mod 24).
For all integers n, if n is prime and n > 3 then n^2 ≡ 1 (mod 16) or n^2 ≡ 9 (mod 16).
For all integers n, if n is prime and n > 3 then n ≡ 1 or 2 (mod 3).
For all integers n, if n is prime and n > 3 then n^4 ≡ 1 (mod 3).
For all integers n, if n is prime and n > 3 then n - 1 and n + 1 are both even with one divisible by 4.
For all integers n, if n is prime and n > 5 then n^2 - 1 is divisible by 24.
For all integers n, if n is prime and n > 5 then n^4 ≡ 1 (mod 24).
For all integers n, if n is prime and n > 5 then n^3 ≡ n (mod 24).
For all integers n, if n is prime and n > 3 then n^4 - 1 is divisible by 24.
For all integers n, if n is prime and n > 3 then n^2 - 1 divides n^4 - 1.
For all integers n, if n is prime and n > 5 then n^6 ≡ 1 (mod 24).
For all integers n, if n is prime and n > 3 then phi(n^2) ≡ n(n-1) ≡ 0 (mod 2).
For all integers n, if n is prime and n > 3 then sigma(n) = n + 1.
For all integers n, if n is prime and n > 3 then there exist integers a, b such that n = 8a ± 1 = 3b ± 1.
predicate: (not is_prime(n)) or (n <= 3) or ((n % 8 == 1 or n % 8 == 7) and (n % 3 == 1 or n % 3 == 2))

claim: For all integers n, if n is prime and n > 3 then n^2 ≡ 1 (mod 3) implies gcd(n^2 - 1, 3) = 3.
For all integers n, if n is prime and n > 3 then n^2 - 1 is divisible by 6.
For all integers n, if n is prime and n > 3 then one of n - 1 or n + 1 is divisible by 3.
For all integers n, if n is prime and n > 3 then gcd(n^2 - 1, 24) = 24.
For all integers n, if n is prime and n > 3 then n^4 ≡ 1 (mod 8).
For all integers n, if n is prime and n > 3 then n^4 ≡ 1 (mod 3) and n^4 ≡ 1 (mod 8).
For all integers n, if n is prime and n > 3 then n^2 has exactly one primitive root modulo 24.
For all integers n, if n ≡ ±1 (mod 8) and n ≡ ±1 (mod 3) then n^2 ≡ 1 (mod 24).
For all integers n, if n is prime and n > 3 then gcd(n - 1, n + 1) = 2.
For all integers n, if n is prime and n > 3 then the multiplicative order of n modulo 24 divides 2.
For all integers n, if n is prime and n > 3 then n ≡ ±1, ±3 (mod 8).
For all integers n, if n is prime and n > 3 and n ≡ 3 (mod 8) then n^2 ≡ 9 (mod 16).
For all integers n, if n is prime and n > 3 then n^2 ≡ 1 or 9 (mod 16) based on n ≡ ±1 or ±3 (mod 8).
For all integers n, if n is prime and n > 3 then n^2 - 1 is always a product of consecutive even numbers.
For all integers n, if n is prime and n > 3 then at least one of n - 1, n + 1 is divisible by 6.
For all integers n, if n is prime and n > 5 then n^2 - 1 is divisible by 12.
For all integers n, if n is prime and n > 5 then n divides n^3 and n^3 - n is divisible by n.
For all integers n, if n is prime and n > 5 then n^12 ≡ 1 (mod 24).
For all integers n, if n is prime and n > 3 then n^2 ≡ 1 (mod 24) means n is coprime to 24.
For all integers n, if n is prime and n > 5 then n^2 - 1 is divisible by 12 but n^2 - 1 ≡ 0 (mod 24) or 12 (mod 24).
For all integers n, if n is prime and n > 3 then the set of quadratic residues modulo 24 containing n^2 has order dividing 2.
For all integers n, if n is prime and n > 5 then n^lcm(4,6,12) ≡ 1 (mod 24) where lcm(4,6,12) = 12.
For all integers n, if n is prime and n > 3 then n ≢ 0 (mod 3) and n ≡ 1 or 2 (mod 3).
For all integers n, if n is prime and n > 3 then n modulo 24 generates a subgroup of order dividing 2 in (Z/24Z)*.
For all integers n, if n is prime and n > 5 then n^2 - 1 ≡ 0 (mod 12) and divisible by 2 means divisible by 24.
For all integers n, if n is prime and n > 3 then (n-1)/gcd(n-1,n+1) and (n+1)/gcd(n-1,n+1) are coprime odd numbers.
predicate: (not is_prime(n)) or (n <= 3) or (gcd((n - 1) // gcd(n - 1, n + 1), (n + 1) // gcd(n - 1, n + 1)) == 1)

claim: For all integers n, if n is prime and n > 3 then n generates a subgroup of (Z/24Z)* of order exactly 2.
For all integers n, if n is prime and n ≥ 5 then 24 divides n^2 - 1 exactly once.
For all integers n, if n is prime and n > 3 then the product (n-1)(n+1) = n^2 - 1 is composed of complementary factors modulo 2.
